plan_title: Validate shader compilation before scene activation

evaluation.summary: The plan adds a validation layer (2 new files, 4 modified) that intercepts shader compilation errors at startup. It uses existing Three.js APIs (renderer.debug.onShaderError, renderer.compile) and threads an error collector through the existing dependency chain. The architectural footprint is small: one new interface method on ModeManager, one new optional field on LoopDeps, and a widened return type on initScene. No new external dependencies, no changes to the rendering pipeline itself, and no impact on runtime performance after the one-time startup validation.

new_abstractions.description: ShaderErrorCollector class and validateShaderCompilation utility. Both are narrowly scoped and don't introduce new patterns foreign to the codebase.

interface_changes.initScene_return_type: Widened to include errorCollector — backward-compatible addition
interface_changes.ModeManager_interface: Two new methods (initAllForValidation, cleanupInactive) — extends existing interface without breaking it

architectural_analysis.dependency_flow: scene.ts creates collector → main.ts passes it to LoopDeps → renderLoop.ts passes it to validation utility. Clean unidirectional flow, no circular dependencies.
architectural_analysis.coupling_assessment: Low. The validation layer reads from a shared collector but does not modify renderer behavior. The onShaderError callback is append-only. The duck-type check in renderLoop for initAllForValidation avoids hard-coupling to ModeManager.

technical_risks.notes: renderer.debug.onShaderError was introduced in r149 and is stable through r183. The plan correctly identifies this API.

renderer.compile() forces synchronous program compilation in Three.js. The onShaderError callback fires during WebGLProgram creation, which happens synchronously within compile(). This is well-documented behavior.

technical_risks.risk: initAllForValidation temporarily adding all systems to scene
technical_risks.notes: Plan correctly sets opacity 0 on non-active systems and cleans up after validation. Validation happens before first visible render frame (the loop is already running but only rendering the placeholder at this point).

technical_risks.risk: cleanup() disposing geometry that mode transitions need to re-create
technical_risks.notes: Mode transitions already call init() on incoming systems (modeManager.ts:103), which fully re-creates geometry. This is the existing contract.

concerns.detail: The validation call is placed in the per-frame callback (lines 162-164), gated by the one-time init check. This means renderer.compile() and validation run synchronously during a rAF frame, which could cause a single long frame. This is acceptable since it happens exactly once, but the plan should note this.

The plan throws an Error on shader compilation failure. In the current code, this throw would happen inside the rAF callback, which means it becomes an uncaught promise rejection / unhandled error. The plan should consider whether to catch this in renderLoop or let it propagate. Given this is a dev-time gate (definition-of-done), throwing is appropriate — it will show in the console as a fatal error.

createModeManager in main.ts receives pre-created system instances wrapped in factory functions that just return them (e.g., factory: () => particles). The systems array in modeManager.ts calls m.factory() once at creation. So initAllForValidation calling init() on all systems is correct — the system objects already exist.

compatibility.existing_tests: modeManager.test.ts exists and will need updates for the new methods. No conflicts with existing test patterns.
compatibility.build_system: No changes to Vite config or build pipeline.
compatibility.runtime_behavior: No observable changes when shaders compile successfully. Only adds a startup gate that throws on failure.
compatibility.three_js_version: All APIs used (renderer.debug.onShaderError, renderer.compile) are available in Three.js r183.

decision: Plan is architecturally sound. Low impact, well-scoped, uses stable Three.js APIs, and follows existing codebase patterns. No ADR required. Approved for implementation.
